unit.name: Airlock / Pass-Through Chamber System
unit.cfj: Control material and personnel transfer between classified zones without breaching pressure cascade or contamination boundary

meta.method: Ulwick ODI (Importance × Satisfaction → Opportunity), needs scoring v2

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that loss of power, control communication, or pressure input leaves the airlock in a cascade-breaching state
rows.imp_rat: A fail state that leaves the airlock cascade-breaching on power/control loss is a critical safe-state failure; central to the CFJ and severe because it defeats containment when least expected.
rows.sat_rat: Fail-safe defaults exist as good practice but coverage of all combined loss modes is inconsistent, leaving credible cascade-breaching states. Defining fail-safe behavior across power, comms and pressure-loss cases substantially extends the specification effort. Requires failure-mode reasoning and fail-safe design judgement that goes beyond routine interlock wiring and is unevenly applied. Fail-safe hardware such as UPS, spring-return actuators or de-energize-to-lock adds real cost that projects sometimes trim.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that emergency release behavior creates an uncontrolled contamination-boundary breach
rows.imp_rat: Emergency release creating an uncontrolled breach undermines containment precisely when hazards are elevated; relevant to safe-state design and severe when triggered.
rows.sat_rat: Emergency release routinely defaults to opening for life safety, creating an acknowledged and poorly-mitigated contamination-breach pathway. Reconciling emergency egress requirements with containment adds significant analysis and negotiation time. Balancing life-safety override against contamination boundary demands rare cross-disciplinary judgement between safety and containment experts. Engineered solutions like interlocked emergency paths or airlocked egress are costly and often deferred.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of contamination ingress events into a higher-classified zone during a cascade breach
rows.imp_rat: Contamination ingress into a higher-classified zone during a breach is the exact harm the CFJ prevents; both relevance and severity are near maximal.
rows.sat_rat: Properly designed and maintained cascades make ingress uncommon, though breaches during transfers still produce occasional ingress that is hard to fully confirm or exclude. Pressure cascade responds instantly to breaches, but detection-to-response time for ingress varies. Interpreting whether a cascade breach caused ingress requires trained assessment beyond the automated system. Cascade design and HEPA infrastructure are capital-heavy but standard; ingress events add remediation cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an alarm reset permits transfer while the triggering breach remains unresolved
rows.imp_rat: An alarm reset permitting transfer while the breach persists lets contamination continue while masking the fault; central to breach handling and severe due to compounded undetected exposure.
rows.sat_rat: Alarm resets frequently clear permissive locks without verifying the underlying breach is resolved, a common latent failure. Linking alarm reset to actual breach resolution adds modest logic and validation time. Ensuring a reset cannot re-enable transfer while the fault persists requires careful state-machine design often done sloppily. Purely a logic feature with negligible cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of defined fault scenarios in which control logic fails to reach the specified safe state
rows.imp_rat: Fault scenarios where logic fails to reach safe state leave containment defeated during faults; central to fail-safe design and severe because protection is lost when most needed.
rows.sat_rat: Safe-state coverage exists where rigorous analysis is done but many designs leave fault scenarios without verified safe-state resolution. Enumerating fault scenarios and proving safe-state arrival is a time-intensive analytical exercise. Systematic FMEA/HAZOP-style safe-state verification demands specialist safety-engineering skill unevenly available on projects. Formal fault analysis consumes engineering hours that lower-tier projects underfund.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of power, pressure-input, or control-communication loss conditions lacking a defined safe state
rows.imp_rat: Loss conditions lacking a defined safe state leave the airlock in an undefined, likely breaching state on failure; central to fail-safe design and severe because protection vanishes during the failure.
rows.sat_rat: Power-loss safe states are common but pressure-input and comms-loss states are less consistently specified, leaving recurring gaps. Defining safe states for each utility-loss condition extends design and commissioning effort. Requires deliberate loss-of-utility failure analysis that is standard on rigorous jobs but inconsistently applied elsewhere. Backup power and fail-safe actuation add real cost that is sometimes value-engineered out.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an authorized transfer procedure permits a contamination-boundary breach
rows.imp_rat: A procedure that itself permits a boundary breach is a systematic latent defect affecting every execution; relevance high, severity high due to repeated exposure.
rows.sat_rat: Latent procedural flaws that permit breaches slip through review with some regularity because validation is judgement-based rather than systematically verified. Procedure validation and review cycles are slow, delaying detection of contamination-permitting flaws. Whether an authorized procedure inadvertently permits a boundary breach depends heavily on the expertise of the procedure author and reviewers, a scarce and error-prone judgement. Expert review is costly, and a flawed procedure that ships causes expensive contamination events.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an authorized transfer procedure permits loss of the required pressure cascade
rows.imp_rat: A procedure permitting cascade loss is a systematic defect embedded in operations; high relevance to the job with high consequence across repeated use.
rows.sat_rat: Because it relies on expert foresight during validation, procedures that inadvertently allow cascade loss still get approved with recurring frequency. Reviewing procedures for cascade-loss risk is a slow, periodic activity rather than continuous. Detecting that a procedure permits pressure-cascade loss requires deep process and HVAC expertise that is scarce among procedure reviewers. Expert validation resources are costly and a cascade-permitting procedure risks batch loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of directional airflow reversal across the classified-zone boundary during a transfer
rows.imp_rat: Directional airflow reversal across the boundary is a definitive cascade breach carrying contaminants; highly central with severe consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Pressure cascades reliably drive direction under steady state, but during door opening the airflow direction is turbulent and harder to guarantee, leaving a real gap. Airflow-direction verification relies on periodic smoke studies and monitoring rather than continuous instant feedback, so confirmation lags. Predicting and specifying directional airflow behavior across an opening boundary demands experienced airflow-visualization interpretation. Smoke studies and airflow monitoring add moderate recurring cost but are routine.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a manual action bypasses required interlock state sequencing
rows.imp_rat: A manual action bypassing interlock sequencing defeats the primary safeguard; central to the CFJ and severe because it enables the worst breach through human override.
rows.sat_rat: Manual actions and maintenance modes remain a frequent route around sequencing, so residual bypass risk persists. Adding sequence-enforcement to manual actions requires modest additional logic-design time. Preventing manual bypass of interlock sequencing depends on disciplined design plus operator training, both variably applied. Enforced sequencing is largely logic with little hardware cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of air crossing from the lower-classified zone into the higher-classified zone during a transfer
rows.imp_rat: Air crossing from lower to higher classification is precisely the contamination event the boundary must stop; direct realization of the failure the CFJ guards against.
rows.sat_rat: Pressure cascades reliably bias airflow inward, but every door opening still admits some back-flow that current practice controls only partially. Pressure cascade acts continuously so there is no time penalty, but recovery lags after each door cycle. Cascade design is engineered in, yet correct interpretation of transient air movement during transfer still needs some operator awareness. Maintaining cascade airflow consumes significant continuous HVAC energy and filtration cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that control logic permits transfer while zone pressure is outside acceptance limits
rows.imp_rat: Control logic permitting transfer while pressure is out of limits directly authorizes a breach; central logic defect with severe contamination consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Pressure-permissive transfer is common practice yet band definition and drift handling leave recurring authorization gaps. Adding pressure-window acceptance to transfer permissives extends tuning and validation modestly. Defining acceptable pressure bands and enforcing them requires competent but conventional controls work. Uses existing pressure instrumentation with limited added cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of override paths capable of defeating contamination-boundary protection
rows.imp_rat: Override paths that can defeat boundary protection are latent bypasses of the entire containment function; central to the CFJ and severe because a single misuse causes a full breach.
rows.sat_rat: Overrides for maintenance and emergencies routinely exist and are inconsistently governed, leaving containment-defeating paths in many designs. Auditing and constraining override paths adds review time to the specification. Recognizing and closing every override capable of defeating containment requires disciplined security-minded design judgement that is scarce. Restricting overrides is largely a logic-design choice with little hardware cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that invalid zone or boundary status is treated as permissive
rows.imp_rat: Treating invalid status as permissive is a fail-permissive logic defect that lets transfers proceed without real assurance; central and severe due to undetected breach potential.
rows.sat_rat: Default behavior frequently interprets absent or invalid status as permissive rather than restrictive, making this a common latent hazard. Building explicit invalid-status handling into permissives adds meaningful design and verification effort. Treating invalid status as non-permissive requires deliberate fail-safe state design that many implementations neglect. Needs status-quality signaling and diagnostic logic that add modest but often-omitted cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that both zone-facing boundaries are open simultaneously during a transfer
rows.imp_rat: Both zone-facing boundaries open simultaneously is the definitive interlock breach opening a direct path; central to the CFJ with severe cross-zone consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Door interlocks are a mature, near-universal feature that makes simultaneous opening a very rare event, one of the best-solved needs here. Simultaneous-opening prevention is enforced instantly by interlocks, with no time cost to the role. Specifying door interlocks is a standard, low-skill requirement with well-known off-the-shelf solutions. Mechanical or electronic interlocks are inexpensive and standard equipment.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of pressure reversal between classified zones during a transfer
rows.imp_rat: Pressure reversal between zones is a direct cascade breach that drives contaminant flow the wrong direction; both centrality and consequence to the CFJ are high.
rows.sat_rat: Interlocked doors and continuous DP monitoring are mature and widely deployed, though transient reversals during rapid door cycling remain a known residual risk. Verifying no reversal occurs during a transfer is handled by interlock logic that acts in real time, so no delay is imposed on the role. Specifying reversal-proof interlocks and differential targets requires solid cascade design competence but follows well-known patterns. Reversal protection via interlocks and monitoring is a standard cost item already budgeted in airlock systems.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of pressure-separation loss between classified zones during transfer activity
rows.imp_rat: Duration of pressure-separation loss defines the contaminant-transfer exposure window; central to the CFJ with severity scaling by lapse length.
rows.sat_rat: Well-designed airlocks recover pressure quickly and predictably, but transfer activity still creates unavoidable windows of separation loss. The core error is duration of separation loss, and recovery time after door cycling is only partly minimized by fast-acting HVAC. Reducing this duration depends on system design rather than operator skill. Faster recovery requires higher HVAC capacity and tighter chamber design, raising cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of contamination spread from the affected chamber into cleaner zones
rows.imp_rat: Contamination spread from the affected chamber into cleaner zones is the direct escalation the boundary must contain; central to the CFJ with severe consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Once a chamber is compromised, containment relies on manual response and there is little automatic barrier, so spread is inconsistently limited. Containing spread after an event triggers time-consuming isolation and cleanup response. Limiting spread depends on correct emergency judgement and procedure execution under pressure. Decontamination and downtime from a spread event are costly.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of material transfers involving residual surface contamination entering a cleaner classified zone
rows.imp_rat: Residual surface contamination entering a cleaner zone defeats the transfer-control purpose; a real contamination event though localized to transferred items, keeping severity high but not maximal.
rows.sat_rat: Residual surface contamination frequently slips through because manual wipe-down is inconsistent and rarely verified in real time, leaving this a routine weak point. Surface disinfection and wipe-down add measurable dwell time before each transfer can proceed. Effective decontamination depends heavily on manual technique and operator discipline, making it error-prone. Sporicidal agents, wipes, and validated dwell procedures add recurring consumable cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an invalid or stale pressure indication authorizes a transfer transition
rows.imp_rat: A stale/invalid pressure indication authorizing a transition means decisions rest on false state, silently defeating the interlock; central to reliable control with severe consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Most simple airlock logic trusts the last reading without validity checks, so stale-signal authorization is a real and under-addressed failure mode. Adding signal validity, staleness and range checking to permissive logic notably increases specification and test time. Detecting stale or invalid sensor data requires instrumentation-diagnostic expertise that basic interlock designs commonly omit. Smart transmitters and diagnostic logic add cost that budget designs frequently skip.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that opposing chamber doors are open simultaneously during entry
rows.imp_rat: Opposing chamber doors open simultaneously during entry directly opens a contamination channel; central interlock failure with severe cross-zone consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Interlocks reliably block simultaneous door opening on entry, with residual risk only from bypass, propping, or hardware faults. Entry sequencing through interlocks imposes a minor wait but keeps throughput acceptable. The interlock enforces correct entry sequence automatically, requiring little operator judgement. No meaningful added cost beyond the standard interlock already fitted.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of simultaneous opposing-door openings during transfers
rows.imp_rat: Simultaneous opposing-door openings are the direct interlock breach the airlock must prevent; frequency reduction is central with severe contamination consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Two-door interlocks are a mature, near-universal standard practice that reliably blocks simultaneous opening, so this rarely surfaces where fitted. Mechanical/electronic interlocks impose only the unavoidable sequential open-close delay with no extra executor effort. Interlocks physically prevent both doors opening, so the executor needs almost no vigilance to satisfy this need. Interlock hardware is a modest one-time capital cost with low maintenance.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that opposing chamber doors are open concurrently during transfer
rows.imp_rat: Opposing chamber doors open concurrently is the fundamental interlock breach directly eliminating the boundary; consequences are severe cross-zone contamination.
rows.sat_rat: Door interlocks are a mature, near-ubiquitous engineering control that reliably prevents simultaneous door opening except during override or failure. Interlocks force a brief mandatory wait before the second door releases, a small predictable delay. Mechanical or electronic interlocks remove reliance on operator discipline, so almost no skill is needed to prevent concurrent opening. Interlock hardware is a standard built-in feature with negligible marginal operating cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that opposing access barriers are open simultaneously during a transfer
rows.imp_rat: Simultaneous opening of opposing barriers directly defeats the airlock's core containment function, creating a direct open path between zones; consequences are severe cross-contamination and pressure-cascade loss.
rows.sat_rat: Door interlocks are a mature, near-universal engineered control that makes concurrent opening a rare failure, though bypass, override, and interlock faults leave a residual gap. Mechanical and electronic interlocks act instantly, adding no meaningful delay to a transfer while blocking simultaneous opening. Interlocking removes reliance on operator vigilance almost entirely, so little skill is required for this specific error. Interlock hardware is standard and inexpensive relative to the airlock system as a whole.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that both airlock boundary doors are open concurrently during a transfer
rows.imp_rat: Both boundary doors open concurrently is the canonical interlock failure that voids the contamination boundary; the breach is immediate and can be catastrophic for the higher-classified zone.
rows.sat_rat: Door interlocks are mature, widely deployed status-quo practice that make concurrent open-door events rare, though defeat, bypass, or interlock failure still occasionally occurs. Mechanical or electrical interlocks act instantly, adding no meaningful delay to the transfer sequence the overseer governs. Interlocks remove reliance on operator judgement to avoid dual-door opening, so little skill is required once the system is validated. Interlock hardware is standard and inexpensive relative to the contamination events it prevents.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that barrier release occurs before the required pressure differential is established
rows.imp_rat: Releasing a barrier before the pressure differential is established directly opens the zone without protection; this sequencing error is central and highly consequential.
rows.sat_rat: Pressure-interlocked doors are established practice but sensor placement, thresholds and hysteresis leave recurring gaps that reduce certainty. Pressure-permissive interlock design takes modest additional engineering time to tie sensors to release logic. Requires the engineer to correctly integrate differential-pressure sensing into interlock logic, a somewhat more demanding but common competency. Adds differential-pressure sensors and control tie-ins at moderate, routinely-budgeted cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a subsequent barrier release occurs before the pressure cascade recovers
rows.imp_rat: Releasing a subsequent barrier before cascade recovery reopens the zone prematurely; central sequencing control with high but often recoverable consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Recovery is frequently handled by fixed delays rather than verified pressure restoration, leaving real recurring gaps in true cascade-recovery assurance. Adding cascade-recovery timing or confirmation logic lengthens commissioning and tuning effort. Correctly specifying recovery confirmation before a subsequent release demands nuanced judgement about cascade dynamics that many designs handle crudely. Often relies on simple timers rather than measured recovery, keeping cost low but at the expense of rigor.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that opposing airlock boundaries open concurrently during a transfer
rows.imp_rat: Concurrent opening of opposing boundaries is the primary interlock failure the airlock must prevent; the resulting open channel makes severity high.
rows.sat_rat: This restates the widely-solved concurrent-opening problem, so status quo interlocking serves it well though edge cases with multi-boundary chambers add slight residual risk. Specifying opposing-boundary interlocks follows the same established workflow and adds little time. The concept mirrors the standard two-door interlock and requires only conventional controls knowledge. Reuses the same interlock hardware and logic, imposing no meaningful additional cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of personnel or material entering a classified zone without confirmed release status
rows.imp_rat: Entry without confirmed release status lets unverified material/personnel into a classified zone; central to controlled transfer with severe contamination potential.
rows.sat_rat: Status confirmation is often procedural and manual, so unreleased material or personnel can enter when checks are skipped or misread. Release-status verification adds procedural checks that slow the transfer. Confirming release status relies on operator diligence and correct paperwork or system checks, which are error-prone. Electronic release systems cost more; paper-based checks are cheap but weak.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that items enter a cleaner classified zone without verified transfer status
rows.imp_rat: Items entering a cleaner zone without verified transfer status bypass the control the airlock provides; central to controlled transfer with high contamination consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Status verification is procedurally required but manually enforced, so unverified items occasionally enter cleaner zones. Verifying transfer status adds a check step but is generally brief. Confirming verified transfer status relies on operator adherence to labeling/logging conventions rather than automated gating. Labeling and status-tag systems are cheap but add handling overhead.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that reset or restart restores access before required boundary conditions are reestablished
rows.imp_rat: Reset/restart restoring access before boundary conditions are reestablished reopens the zone prematurely; central to safe recovery with high consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Restart behavior is inconsistently constrained, and premature access on reset is a real recurring gap in many implementations. Specifying condition-gated restart adds logic-design and testing time. Requires deliberate restart-sequencing judgement to avoid premature access, a competent but often-overlooked skill. Implemented in control logic at low incremental cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that both airlock barriers are open concurrently during a transfer
rows.imp_rat: Concurrent barrier opening is central to the CFJ since it eliminates the separation the airlock exists to provide; severity is high due to unmitigated contamination transfer.
rows.sat_rat: Dual-door interlocks are mature, ubiquitous and highly reliable, making concurrent opening a rare and well-mitigated event where standard practice is applied. Standard electrical/mechanical interlock design is a well-understood engineering task that adds negligible time to specification. Mutual-exclusion interlocking is textbook cleanroom/GMP practice that most controls engineers can specify without specialist expertise. Interlock hardware and logic are inexpensive and universally included in airlock packages, so cost is a non-issue.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of a transfer beginning while either adjacent zone is outside its required classification state
rows.imp_rat: Beginning a transfer while an adjacent zone is out of classification state means transferring into/out of a non-conforming environment; central to controlled transfer with high consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Some interlocks tie to pressure state, but classification recovery (particle counts, cleanup time) is frequently assumed rather than confirmed, leaving real gaps. Verifying both zones are in required state before transfer adds a brief check. Judging zone readiness requires operator awareness of classification recovery status. Monitoring instrumentation for zone state adds moderate cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of dismissing a pressure-cascade reversal as normal fluctuation
rows.imp_rat: Dismissing a cascade reversal as normal fluctuation lets a real breach go unaddressed; relevant to detection with severe consequence because the breach continues unrecognized.
rows.sat_rat: With simple gauges and fixed alarms, real reversals are commonly dismissed as fluctuation, leaving this weakly served. Recognizing a reversal versus noise takes no measurable time but relies on the operator noticing. Distinguishing a genuine cascade reversal from normal fluctuation demands experienced judgement that novices often lack, so the need is skill-bound. Better trend analytics or smart alarms cost more and are rarely deployed.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the specified differential cannot be maintained because expected leakage paths or transfer disturbances were omitted
rows.imp_rat: If expected leakage paths are omitted so the differential can't be maintained, the boundary silently fails under real conditions; a systemic design gap central to reliable containment.
rows.sat_rat: There is no comprehensive standard checklist for all leakage and transfer disturbances, so omissions are a recurring cause of unmaintainable differentials. Accounting for leakage paths and transfer disturbances requires added analysis cycles that extend the specification phase. Anticipating every leakage path and dynamic disturbance is highly expertise-dependent and often relies on the individual engineer's foresight. Omitted disturbances surface as field failures needing costly retrofits and rebalancing.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of a boundary breach producing no discernible indication during transfer
rows.imp_rat: A breach producing no discernible indication means contamination proceeds undetected; relevant to detection reliability and severe due to silent, unremediated exposure.
rows.sat_rat: Many breaches produce no discernible indication with typical instrumentation, so this remains a significant undetected-failure gap. Detecting a silent breach adds no time because it typically isn't detected in real time at all. Absent instrumentation the executor must infer a breach from indirect cues, demanding vigilance that often fails. Continuous breach-detection sensing is expensive and seldom installed.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of contaminant passage across the breached transfer boundary
rows.imp_rat: Duration of contaminant passage across a breached boundary determines total contaminant transfer; central to containment with severity tied to exposure length.
rows.sat_rat: Interlocks bound the worst case but the actual open duration varies with executor pace, leaving meaningful variability. Minimizing how long a breach stays open depends on fast door cycling and quick executor action, which today is largely manual and variable. The executor must move briskly and close promptly, so outcome depends on trained habit and attention. No significant material cost attaches to shortening breach duration.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that control logic enables opposing access points simultaneously
rows.imp_rat: Control logic that enables opposing access points simultaneously is the root-cause defect behind the worst interlock breach; highly central and severe.
rows.sat_rat: Software plus hardwired interlocks reliably block simultaneous enabling, a well-established and dependable practice. Logic-level mutual exclusion is quick to specify within standard PLC/control frameworks. Preventing simultaneous access-point enable is core interlock logic within routine controls competency. Implemented in existing controllers at negligible incremental cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of credible fault states lacking defined boundary-safe behavior
rows.imp_rat: Credible fault states lacking defined boundary-safe behavior create undefended failure modes; relevant to fail-safe completeness and severe because such faults breach containment unhandled.
rows.sat_rat: Undefined boundary behavior for less-obvious fault states remains a common gap outside high-rigor projects. Identifying credible fault states and defining boundary-safe behavior for each is a lengthy analytical task. Requires thorough failure-mode enumeration expertise that routine airlock specs seldom fully apply. Comprehensive analysis and the resulting mitigations add engineering and possibly hardware cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of airborne contaminant escape from the chamber during opening of a zone-facing boundary
rows.imp_rat: Airborne contaminant escape when a zone-facing boundary opens directly transfers contamination out of the chamber; central to the CFJ with high consequence for adjacent zones.
rows.sat_rat: Interlocks and pressure cascades limit escape, but the moment of boundary opening is an inherently weak point that standard practice only partially controls. Contaminant-escape risk at door opening is evaluated via smoke studies that take scheduling and rework time to iterate. Interpreting escape behavior during dynamic opening requires specialized contamination-control expertise. Mitigations like air showers, overspill air or dynamic passthroughs add meaningful capital cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of airflow reversals toward the higher-classification zone during transfer
rows.imp_rat: Airflow reversal toward the higher-classification zone is a core cascade breach; frequency reduction is central though a brief reversal may be partially recoverable.
rows.sat_rat: Well-designed cascade HVAC with monitoring keeps reversals uncommon, but transient reversals during door cycling still slip through, leaving residual failures. Interlocked pressure cascades act continuously so no added time is imposed on the executor during transfer. Mature differential-pressure gauges and alarms mean the executor needs little judgement to detect a reversal, though reading gauges still demands attention. Fan and control-system energy plus periodic gauge calibration are ongoing but modest recurring costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of pressure-cascade deviation from the required zone relationship during transfer
rows.imp_rat: Pressure-cascade deviation from the required relationship directly undermines the boundary-preserving mechanism; deviations can allow contaminant migration though often recoverable, tempering severity slightly.
rows.sat_rat: Magnehelic gauges, alarms and pressure-hold designs make cascade maintenance a mature and largely dependable practice, though door cycling and leaks still cause occasional excursions. Pressure recovery between transfers takes a defined settling period that the operator generally waits out, adding modest but tolerated delay. Modern airlocks automate the cascade so the operator relies on gauges and indicator lights rather than personal judgement, lowering the skill burden. Maintaining the cascade consumes continuous HVAC energy and periodic recalibration, an accepted recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that loss of the required pressure relationship between classified zones is not identified as a boundary breach
rows.imp_rat: Failing to identify loss of the pressure relationship as a breach means the containment failure goes unrecognized and unremediated; central to the CFJ and severe due to undetected exposure.
rows.sat_rat: Continuous pressure monitoring with alarms reliably flags most pressure losses as breaches, though sensor drift and unmonitored transients leave a residual detection gap. Differential pressure gauges and alarms indicate loss essentially in real time. Interpreting a pressure alarm as a boundary breach is fairly straightforward with standard training. DP sensors, magnehelic gauges, and monitoring add moderate but well-established cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the required pressure hierarchy between adjacent classified zones is specified in the wrong direction
rows.imp_rat: Specifying the pressure hierarchy in the wrong direction is a systemic design error that inverts protection for every transfer; less frequent to occur but catastrophic when it does.
rows.sat_rat: Direction is defined by well-established GMP cascade conventions and reviewed in P&ID/QRA steps, so outright reversed hierarchies are rare though occasionally missed in complex adjacencies. Confirming the correct hierarchy direction is a quick design-review checkpoint against the classification scheme. Getting cascade direction right (containment vs. cleanliness logic) requires knowing whether the zone is protecting product or personnel, which trips up less-experienced specifiers. A wrong-direction error caught only after build is extremely expensive, but the check itself costs nothing.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of viable contamination remaining on transfer-contact surfaces after cleaning
rows.imp_rat: Viable contamination remaining on transfer-contact surfaces after cleaning defeats decontamination; relevant to controlled transfer with high consequence for the cleaner zone.
rows.sat_rat: Manual surface cleaning frequently leaves viable residue with no in-line verification, making this a chronic, poorly served pain. Effective cleaning requires disinfectant contact/dwell time that adds delay. Achieving true viable-load reduction demands correct agent, coverage and dwell — highly technique-dependent and easy to shortcut. Disinfectants and validated cleaning consumables are recurring costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of mistaking incomplete chamber closure for complete closure
rows.imp_rat: Mistaking incomplete closure for complete closure lets the interlock proceed on a false-closed door; central detection defect with severe consequence akin to a concurrent-opening breach.
rows.sat_rat: Position/seal sensors catch most incomplete closures, but partial seating that still satisfies a switch can be misread as complete, leaving a residual gap. Closure confirmation via sensors is immediate and adds no executor time. Sensor feedback reduces reliance on the executor visually judging complete closure. Closure-sensing hardware is a bounded one-time cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of boundary crossings made while the chamber pressure state is outside acceptance criteria
rows.imp_rat: Crossings while chamber pressure is outside acceptance directly move material/personnel through an unprotected boundary state; central to controlled transfer with high consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Interlocks and indicators help, but crossings during marginal or out-of-spec pressure still happen when alarms are muted or overridden. Waiting for pressure to return to acceptance before crossing adds delay that operators under production pressure sometimes shortcut. Operators must read and interpret pressure status correctly before crossing, placing real dependence on attention and training. Verification consumes little resource but a bad crossing can force costly re-cleaning or batch loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of contamination carried into the cleaner zone by personnel during passage
rows.imp_rat: Contamination carried into the cleaner zone by personnel is a direct realization of transfer failure; relevant to controlled personnel transfer with high consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Human-borne contamination control is behavior-driven and inconsistent, making this one of the least reliably served needs. Gowning and personnel decon steps add substantial time before passage. Contamination carried by personnel depends heavily on gowning discipline and correct technique, which is highly skill- and behavior-dependent. Gowning consumables and PPE are significant recurring costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that evaluated control logic differs from approved logic for the designated airlock or pass-through chamber
rows.imp_rat: Evaluating logic that differs from approved logic means the operating control is unverified; relevant to assurance and severe because unqualified logic may contain breaching defects.
rows.sat_rat: Version-control discipline for control logic is inconsistent across projects, so evaluated logic silently diverging from approved logic is a recurring and often undetected failure. Reconciling evaluated logic against the current approved baseline is slow because it depends on hunting through revision histories and change logs that are rarely current. Detecting that evaluated logic has drifted from approved logic demands a seasoned reviewer who remembers prior decisions, since no tool reliably flags the mismatch. The rework and re-review triggered when an unapproved logic version is caught late consumes significant engineering hours.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of releasing a transfer into the receiving zone before chamber conditions recover to approved limits
rows.imp_rat: Releasing a transfer before chamber conditions recover exposes the receiving zone to unrestored conditions; central sequencing gate with high consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Where time-based or pressure-gated release exists it works, but many chambers rely on operator judgement, allowing premature release into the receiving zone. Waiting for chamber recovery before release imposes a real dwell-time penalty. Deciding when conditions have recovered often relies on operator judgement rather than a hard gate. Timed or sensor-gated release adds controls cost but little consumable expense.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unauthorized material or personnel crossings of a classified-zone boundary
rows.imp_rat: Unauthorized boundary crossings compromise controlled transfer, part of the CFJ; severity depends on what crosses, so moderate-high rather than maximal.
rows.sat_rat: Established access control and interlocks cover most cases, yet tailgating, propped doors, and unlogged crossings remain recurring gaps. Access controls and interlocks gate crossings without adding delay, but investigating unauthorized crossings consumes time. Preventing unauthorized crossings leans on procedural discipline and vigilance rather than robust automated identity checks in many airlocks. Badge/access systems are moderately costly but common; the residual manual oversight adds ongoing cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of personnel transfers across the contamination boundary involving compromised gowning
rows.imp_rat: Compromised gowning during personnel transfer introduces contamination across the boundary; relevant to controlled personnel transfer but one contributing factor among several, keeping proxies moderate-high.
rows.sat_rat: Gowning breaches are a leading and persistent source of personnel-borne contamination that current visual checks catch only inconsistently. Gowning and re-gowning consume substantial time at each boundary crossing. Correct gowning is a highly technique-dependent, attention-intensive act that even trained personnel compromise regularly. Gowns, consumables, and repeated qualification carry ongoing cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of next-door opening before chamber pressure stabilizes
rows.imp_rat: Opening the next door before chamber pressure stabilizes releases into an unrecovered state; a central sequencing error with high consequence for the receiving zone.
rows.sat_rat: Better systems gate on measured stabilization, yet many chambers use fixed timers that can release early, leaving a recurring reliability gap. Pressure-stabilization holds add a short wait before the next door releases. Where interlocks are pressure-linked the executor need not judge readiness, but simple timer-only interlocks push that judgement back onto attention. Sensor-linked interlocks cost more than timers but remain a bounded one-time investment.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount by which the required minimum pressure differential falls below the boundary-preserving threshold during transfer
rows.imp_rat: Falling below the boundary-preserving differential threshold directly weakens the barrier; central to maintaining separation with high consequence when it occurs.
rows.sat_rat: Standard practice specifies DP setpoints with alarms well above the boundary threshold, making dips below it uncommon but not impossible during transients. Minimum-differential thresholds are set once in the spec and monitored continuously, imposing no ongoing time burden. Choosing a defensible minimum DP that survives transfer disturbances takes engineering judgement about setpoint margins. Maintaining higher differentials to preserve margin raises energy and equipment cost modestly.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of residual chamber contamination transferred to materials or personnel during passage
rows.imp_rat: Residual chamber contamination transferred to materials/personnel defeats the cleaning purpose of the pass-through; relevant to controlled transfer with high but item-scoped consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Manual decontamination of contact surfaces is inconsistent and often incomplete, so residual transfer remains a chronic, poorly controlled failure. Wipe-down and dwell steps add time before passage is complete. Residual-contamination control leans heavily on manual wiping technique and thoroughness, which is error-prone. Wipes, disinfectants and consumables are ongoing costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of transfers proceeding with required decontamination or sanitization steps incomplete
rows.imp_rat: Proceeding with incomplete decon/sanitization allows residual contamination to cross; relevant to controlled transfer with high consequence, though a process-adherence issue.
rows.sat_rat: Without hard interlocks tied to decon completion, transfers proceeding with incomplete sanitization remain a recurring deviation. Decon and sanitization steps have defined dwell times, but there is pressure to shortcut them, and verification lags. Confirming completeness of decon steps depends on operator diligence rather than enforced automated checks. Consumables and dwell time cost money and throughput, creating incentive to skip steps.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of a door treated as closed remaining unsecured before opposing-door release
rows.imp_rat: A door treated as closed while actually unsecured before opposing release enables a concealed concurrent breach; central to interlock integrity with severe consequence because the barrier is falsely trusted.
rows.sat_rat: Interlocks with position sensing reliably block opposing-door release until closure is confirmed, though switch faults occasionally misreport. Door-position confirmation is instantaneous via interlock logic with no added time. Position sensors verify closure so the executor need not manually judge whether a door is truly secured. Position-switch hardware is a modest one-time cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the time between detecting a contamination-boundary breach and stopping affected transfers
rows.imp_rat: Time to stop affected transfers after detecting a breach governs how long contamination continues; relevant to breach response and severe because delay multiplies exposure.
rows.sat_rat: Alarm-triggered stops are fairly quick where automated, but many facilities rely on human response, adding variable delay before affected transfers halt. The error is the delay from detection to stopping transfers, which alarms and interlocks can shorten but manual response often lengthens. Responding correctly to a detected breach depends on operator training and escalation procedures. Automated stop-on-alarm interlocks cost more than procedural responses.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of contamination carried into the cleaner zone on material exteriors
rows.imp_rat: Contamination carried into the cleaner zone on material exteriors is a direct transfer-control failure; relevant to material transfer with high consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Manual material decon leaves inconsistent residual contamination on exteriors, a recurring and poorly verified gap. Surface wipe-down of material exteriors adds time before passage. Effectiveness depends on the executor's wiping thoroughness and technique, which varies. Wipes and disinfectants are ongoing consumable costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of unauthorized interlock overrides during chamber access
rows.imp_rat: Unauthorized interlock overrides defeat the primary safeguard against concurrent opening; relevant to interlock integrity and severe because each override risks a full breach.
rows.sat_rat: Restricted-access and logged overrides curb casual bypasses, but shared credentials and normalized workarounds mean unauthorized overrides still recur. Override authorization workflows add a short delay but are designed into the access sequence. Preventing unauthorized overrides relies on access control rather than operator expertise, reducing skill burden. Key/badge-restricted override controls are standard and inexpensive to maintain.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of pressure differential outside approved limits between classified zones during a transfer
rows.imp_rat: Duration of differential outside approved limits governs the exposure window for cascade breach; central to the CFJ with severity tied to lapse length.
rows.sat_rat: Automated pressure control keeps differentials within limits most of the time, with recurring excursions during and after door cycling. The error is time spent outside approved differential, which recovery ramps reduce but do not eliminate. Little operator skill is involved once the pressure control system is set up. Keeping differential in-limit demands robust HVAC controls and consumes energy.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of chamber contamination exceeding the release limit after transfer
rows.imp_rat: Chamber contamination exceeding release limit after transfer risks carrying contamination onward; relevant to material transfer control with high consequence for the cleaner zone.
rows.sat_rat: Without in-chamber measurement the executor works blind to actual contamination levels, so exceedances go undetected — a significant recurring gap. Verifying contamination is below release limit rarely happens in real time, so no time is added during transfer. Judging whether release limits are met usually relies on assumed procedure compliance rather than measurement, demanding judgement without data. Real-time contamination sensing is costly and seldom deployed, so most sites accept the risk instead.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of transfers authorized using pressure limits inconsistent with the applicable classified zones
rows.imp_rat: Authorizing transfers with pressure limits inconsistent with the zones applies wrong criteria to the boundary decision; relevant defect with high consequence for permitted breaches.
rows.sat_rat: Because limit-zone alignment is verified manually against classification documents, mismatches slip through with recurring frequency. Checking that authorized limits match the applicable zones is a manual review step done during procedure setup. Confirming limit-to-zone consistency requires classification knowledge held by a limited set of qualified reviewers. Errors here trigger costly requalification, but the check itself is low direct cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of containment actions redistributing contamination beyond the affected chamber
rows.imp_rat: Containment actions redistributing contamination beyond the affected chamber worsen a breach during response; relevant to breach handling with high consequence for spread.
rows.sat_rat: There is little engineered support for spread-limiting containment, so redistribution during response is a chronic, poorly served failure. Containment response is manual and slow, giving contamination time to spread during cleanup. Containing a breach without spreading it demands high skill and disciplined technique that few operators reliably execute under pressure. Redistributed contamination multiplies decontamination and lost-product costs dramatically.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of confirming readiness with a chamber door not fully closed and sealed
rows.imp_rat: Confirming readiness with a door not fully sealed authorizes transfer over a compromised boundary; central readiness-detection defect with severe consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Mature dual-position/seal-confirmation sensing means a not-fully-sealed door is rarely confirmed ready, though sensor drift and bypasses leave a small residual failure rate. Interlock logic gives an immediate ready/not-ready indication so no manual delay is added before confirming readiness. Door-position sensors and interlocks remove the reliance on an operator's judgement to visually verify a full seal. Sensor-driven interlocks are standard equipment already amortized into these chamber systems, adding little marginal cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of pressure differential below the required threshold during transfer
rows.imp_rat: Duration of differential below threshold governs exposure window for contaminant migration; central to the CFJ, severity scaled by how long the lapse persists.
rows.sat_rat: Interlocks reliably prevent action while below threshold in equipped chambers, but the duration of the dip itself is only partly controlled and recovery lag remains a recurring gap. Pressure recovery introduces a real wait the executor must respect before proceeding, adding cycle time. Interlocks that hold doors until differential is restored offload most of the judgement from the executor. Sustaining pressure differential draws continuous fan energy but no per-transfer material cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of concurrent-opening events at the transfer interface not identified as boundary breaches
rows.imp_rat: Concurrent-opening events not identified as breaches means the worst interlock failure goes unrecognized; relevant to detection with severe consequence due to unremediated exposure.
rows.sat_rat: Electronic interlocks with audit logs reliably record and flag concurrent-opening attempts, though older mechanical systems may not classify them as breaches. Logging concurrent-opening events is automatic on modern interlocks and adds no time. Recognizing a logged event as a breach needs some review skill but is largely systematized. Event-logging capability is a modest add-on to electronic interlock systems.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a transfer begins while baseline differential pressure is outside validated limits
rows.imp_rat: Beginning a transfer with baseline differential outside validated limits starts from an unprotected state; central readiness gate with high consequence for the transfer.
rows.sat_rat: Continuous DP monitoring with alarms is common practice, but reliance on operators to actually check before starting leaves an occasional gap. Baseline pressure checks are quick, typically visible on a gauge or panel before transfer. Reading whether differential pressure is within limits requires minimal skill when displays and alarms exist. Pressure sensors and gauges are standard, low-cost infrastructure.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the protocol accepts a chamber cycle with post-cycle residues above release criteria
rows.imp_rat: Accepting a cycle with residues above release criteria lets contamination pass as clean; relevant to material transfer control and severe because contaminated items reach cleaner zones.
rows.sat_rat: Mature bio-decontamination validation with biological indicators and chemical residue assays substantially reduces false-accept risk, though recovery-efficiency gaps and marginal spots still let occasional above-criteria residues pass. Residue release testing adds cycle-development time but is a scheduled part of validation the influencer already budgets for. Judging whether acceptance limits truly catch above-criteria residues requires seasoned microbiological and analytical expertise that is not evenly distributed across teams. Recovery studies, coupon testing and repeat cycles carry material and lab cost but are accepted as necessary validation spend.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of contaminant migration through unintended air paths around the chamber boundary
rows.imp_rat: Contaminant migration through unintended air paths around the boundary is a bypass of the intended barrier; relevant to containment integrity with high consequence when leakage paths exist.
rows.sat_rat: These hidden paths are hard to predict at specification and are a recurring source of unexpected contamination migration. Finding unintended air paths around the chamber envelope requires field investigation that can drag out commissioning. Detecting bypass leakage through construction gaps depends on experienced troubleshooting rarely fully captured in specs. Unintended paths discovered late require costly sealing and building rework.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of damaged material packaging releasing contaminants during passage
rows.imp_rat: Damaged packaging releasing contaminants during passage introduces contamination into the chamber; relevant to material transfer with high consequence for the receiving zone.
rows.sat_rat: There is little automated detection of packaging damage in the chamber, so a compromised package releasing contaminant is a poorly mitigated failure mode. Inspecting packaging integrity before passage adds handling time that is often skipped under throughput pressure. Detecting a compromised package depends heavily on the operator's diligence and trained eye, which is inconsistent. Robust secondary packaging and inspection consume material and labor without dedicated equipment support.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of contact between personnel or transferred materials and lower-classified surfaces while crossing the boundary
rows.imp_rat: Contact with lower-classified surfaces during crossing introduces contamination but is a lesser, more localized contributor; moderate relevance and severity.
rows.sat_rat: Inadvertent contact with lower-classified surfaces happens often and is largely unmonitored, making this poorly controlled today. Careful movement to avoid touching surfaces slows personnel and material crossing. Avoiding incidental contact relies almost wholly on trained behavior and constant vigilance, which degrades under production pressure. Little direct cost beyond training, though contamination events from contact are expensive when they occur.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of entrants admitted before the required chamber pressure state is restored
rows.imp_rat: Admitting entrants before chamber pressure is restored exposes the receiving zone to an unrecovered state; central sequencing control with high consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Interlocks and ready-lights make this fairly dependable, but override capability and human haste leave a recurring gap. Requiring pressure restoration before admitting entrants introduces a wait that is sometimes bypassed to save time. Depends on the operator recognizing the ready state, a modest attention demand largely aided by indicators. Little direct cost, though premature admission can trigger expensive contamination events.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of leakage through damaged chamber seals or incomplete closures during transfer
rows.imp_rat: Leakage through damaged seals or incomplete closures degrades the physical boundary; relevant to containment integrity with moderate-high consequence depending on leak magnitude.
rows.sat_rat: Preventive maintenance and door-closed sensors catch many seal issues, but gradual seal degradation and incomplete closures still cause undetected leakage between checks. Seal integrity does not add transfer time except during periodic inspection. Detecting damaged or incomplete seals depends on inspection diligence and some expertise. Seal replacement and inspection programs carry modest ongoing maintenance cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of detected pressure excursions remaining unaddressed during transfer activities
rows.imp_rat: Detected excursions left unaddressed prolong the breach window; relevant to breach response with high consequence tied to unresolved duration.
rows.sat_rat: Alarms ensure detection, but time-to-resolution depends on human availability, so unaddressed durations are inconsistent. The core of this need is shortening how long a detected excursion stays unaddressed, and alarm-driven response times vary widely by shift and staffing. Deciding on and executing a response requires trained personnel who may not be immediately available. Rapid response may need on-call staffing, adding cost that is often not fully resourced.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of interface seal-integrity losses not identified during material or personnel transfers
rows.imp_rat: Seal-integrity losses not identified during transfers mean a degraded boundary goes unnoticed; relevant to detection with high consequence due to undetected leakage.
rows.sat_rat: Real-time detection of seal-integrity loss during transfers is poorly covered, so such failures commonly go unidentified until periodic checks. Seal-integrity monitoring during transfer is largely absent, so no time is spent on it and losses go unnoticed. Detecting integrity loss mid-transfer would require expertise or instrumentation rarely present. Continuous seal-integrity monitoring is costly and seldom deployed at pass-throughs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of residual contamination remaining inside the chamber after a transfer
rows.imp_rat: Residual contamination remaining inside the chamber after transfer can affect subsequent cycles; relevant to sustained control with moderate consequence unless it reaches a cleaner zone.
rows.sat_rat: Residual contamination inside chambers is often addressed only by periodic manual cleaning, so accumulation between cleanings is common and unverified. Post-transfer chamber decontamination adds dwell time between cycles. Effective residual removal depends on manual cleaning technique and consistency. Cleaning agents and any active decontamination systems add recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of passage beginning while the receiving zone is outside classification limits
rows.imp_rat: Passage beginning while the receiving zone is out of classification limits moves material into a non-conforming environment; relevant to controlled transfer with high consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Pressure interlocks catch the gross cases, but true classification state (particle counts, recovery) is often not gated in real time, so out-of-limit passages still occur. Waiting for the receiving zone to return to classification (pressure/recovery) adds real dwell time before passage can begin. Judging whether the receiving zone is truly within limits often still leans on operator interpretation of gauges or procedure. Differential-pressure monitoring is cheap, but continuous zone particle classification monitoring adds meaningful instrumentation cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of credible cascade-breaching transitions excluded from acceptance evaluation
rows.imp_rat: Excluding credible cascade-breaching transitions from acceptance evaluation leaves failure modes unqualified; relevant to assurance completeness with high consequence for unassessed breaches.
rows.sat_rat: Acceptance protocols commonly test nominal cases and omit less-obvious breach transitions, leaving coverage gaps. Expanding acceptance testing to cover all credible cascade-breaching transitions lengthens commissioning considerably. Designing comprehensive test coverage of breach transitions requires validation expertise frequently in short supply. Broader acceptance testing consumes qualification hours and resources that projects often compress.

rows.stmt: Minimize the time between occurrence of a boundary breach and classification of the event as a breach
rows.imp_rat: Delay in classifying an event as a breach postpones remediation and prolongs exposure; relevant to breach handling with high consequence tied to detection lag.
rows.sat_rat: Breach classification is frequently retrospective through deviation review, so events sit unclassified for extended periods with inconsistent triggering. The gap between a breach occurring and its formal classification is often long because it depends on review and deviation processes. Classifying an event as a breach requires expert quality judgement and investigation. Investigation and deviation-management processes consume significant quality resources.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that contamination crosses the closed chamber boundary during transfer-related pressure changes
rows.imp_rat: Contamination crossing the closed boundary during pressure changes is a subtle transient breach; relevant to containment integrity with high consequence during transitions.
rows.sat_rat: Interlocks, pressure differentials and leak-rate testing are mature and generally prevent boundary crossing, though dynamic transfer transients remain an area where ingress can slip through. Pressure-cascade and leak verification is a standard, time-bounded qualification activity. Interpreting differential-pressure and airflow behavior during transfer transients needs experienced HVAC/containment engineers. Instrumentation and smoke/leak studies cost money but are routine capital and validation expenses.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of items released before required surface decontamination is complete
rows.imp_rat: Releasing items before required surface decon is complete lets contamination pass as clean; relevant to material transfer control with high consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Reliance on procedural sign-off without measurement means premature releases still occur intermittently. Confirming decon completion before release adds a checkpoint and minor delay. Depends on operator confirming the full procedure was followed, a vigilance burden. Low direct cost but premature release can force costly recovery.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of item surface samples exceeding contamination acceptance criteria before cleaner-zone entry
rows.imp_rat: Surface samples exceeding acceptance before cleaner-zone entry indicate inadequate decon; relevant to material transfer verification with moderate consequence, a detection safeguard.
rows.sat_rat: Routine verification against acceptance criteria is rare in-line, so most transfers proceed without confirmation, leaving this poorly served. Surface sampling and awaiting results adds significant delay when performed at all. Sampling and interpretation require trained personnel and correct technique. Swab tests and lab analysis carry real recurring cost, so they are done sparingly.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of mismatch between specified chamber pressure relationships and the intended room pressure cascade
rows.imp_rat: A mismatch between specified chamber and intended room cascade is a design/spec defect that can propagate contamination if uncaught; it is one step removed from live operation, moderating relevance.
rows.sat_rat: Mature URS templates, EU GMP Annex and cascade schematics catch most mismatches, yet coordination gaps between chamber vendor and facility designer still let discrepancies slip through. Cross-checking chamber pressure relationships against the room cascade during specification takes iterative coordination with HVAC designers, adding schedule time but not blocking. Getting the match right depends heavily on an experienced qualification/CQV engineer who understands cascade logic, so it hinges on scarce judgement. Mismatches caught late trigger costly rework of ductwork and requalification, but the specification effort itself is inexpensive.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of transfers with validated steps omitted or performed out of sequence
rows.imp_rat: Omitted or out-of-sequence validated steps weaken process assurance and may allow contamination; relevant but a procedural-adherence issue with variable consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Paper-checklist reliance means omitted or out-of-sequence steps are among the more common human-error deviations for transfers. Following the validated sequence takes time and can be rushed under production pressure. Correct sequence execution relies on operator memory and training rather than enforced step-gating in most airlocks. Out-of-sequence transfers cause reprocessing and investigation costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a chamber-to-room seal loses boundary integrity under expected pressure conditions
rows.imp_rat: A chamber-to-room seal losing integrity under expected pressure is a boundary-integrity failure under normal conditions; relevant to containment with high consequence when it occurs.
rows.sat_rat: Seal performance under expected DP is well understood and tested, though seals degrade over time creating occasional integrity loss. Seal integrity is confirmed at commissioning and via periodic requalification, imposing scheduled but manageable time. Specifying appropriate seals for the pressure regime follows established component-selection practice. Quality seals and their maintenance are a bounded, budgeted cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a contamination-boundary breach goes undetected during transfer qualification
rows.imp_rat: A breach undetected during qualification means the boundary weakness ships into operation; relevant to assurance with high consequence because it undermines confidence in containment.
rows.sat_rat: Formal qualification (smoke studies, DP mapping) catches many boundary weaknesses, but subtle or intermittent breaches can still escape a point-in-time test. Qualification testing is scheduled and adds no per-transfer time. Breach detection during qualification relies heavily on skilled protocol design and interpretation. Smoke studies, particle counting, and qualification protocols are expensive but standard.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of destabilization in adjacent room pressure cascades during chamber operation
rows.imp_rat: Destabilizing adjacent room cascades during chamber operation can propagate pressure imbalance; relevant to system-wide cascade integrity with moderate, usually transient consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Standard cascades handle steady state well but chamber door cycling still transiently disturbs adjacent rooms, a partially addressed weakness. Assessing cascade destabilization during chamber operation needs dynamic modeling or field balancing that adds time. Predicting transient effects on adjacent rooms requires advanced HVAC dynamics expertise. Destabilization mitigation via fast-response controls or buffer volumes adds cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of a chamber door remaining incompletely closed or unsecured after transfer
rows.imp_rat: A door remaining incompletely closed after transfer leaves a standing boundary gap; relevant to containment integrity with high consequence until detected.
rows.sat_rat: Interlocks generally prevent an unsecured door from persisting, but slow closers, obstructions and unlatched-but-shut states create recurring gaps. Door-latch and closure alarms flag an unsecured door promptly after a transfer without waiting on rounds. Automatic latch/closure detection reduces dependence on an operator remembering to check the door after passage. The closure-monitoring hardware is inexpensive and already present on interlocked chambers.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of airlock state commands conflicting with facility pressure-cascade requirements
rows.imp_rat: State commands conflicting with facility cascade requirements can drive the airlock against the intended pressure regime; relevant design/logic defect with high consequence if executed.
rows.sat_rat: Facility-level cascade integration is done on well-managed projects but local command conflicts still slip through on many. Reconciling airlock commands with facility-wide cascade requirements adds coordination and analysis time. Requires system-level understanding of the whole cascade beyond the single airlock, a broader competency not always present. Mostly a coordination and design effort with limited direct hardware cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that concurrent room activities destabilize the required pressure cascade during transfer
rows.imp_rat: Concurrent room activities destabilizing the cascade during transfer is an external disturbance to boundary maintenance; relevant but generally transient with moderate consequence.
rows.sat_rat: There is little standard mechanism to guarantee cascade stability against uncontrolled concurrent activities, leaving this poorly served. Concurrent room activities are operational events outside the spec, so the role cannot design them away and reacts after the fact. Anticipating how neighboring operations disturb the cascade requires deep operational insight rarely available at specification time. Destabilization from concurrent activity causes deviations and lost batches that are costly.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of item surfaces becoming recontaminated after decontamination and before cleaner-zone entry
rows.imp_rat: Recontamination after decon and before cleaner-zone entry negates the cleaning step; relevant to controlled transfer with moderate-high consequence.
rows.sat_rat: There is almost no engineered barrier against post-decon recontamination, so it depends on technique and fails often in tight chambers. Preventing recontamination requires careful handling that can slow transfer. Avoiding recontact after cleaning demands considerable operator finesse and spatial discipline. Little direct cost beyond occasional re-cleaning.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of pressure differentials falling outside specified limits during expected facility fluctuations
rows.imp_rat: Differentials falling outside limits during expected fluctuations shows the cascade lacks robustness margin; relevant to boundary maintenance with moderate, usually transient consequence.
rows.sat_rat: BMS/EMS monitoring with alarms keeps DP within limits most of the time, but transient facility fluctuations still cause periodic excursions. Response to facility fluctuations is handled continuously by control loops, so no time is imposed on the role. Specifying control robustness against fluctuations needs moderate controls-tuning knowledge. Tighter control and monitoring to hold limits during fluctuation adds moderate cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of interlock override activation beyond the approved activity
rows.imp_rat: Interlock override activation beyond the approved activity extends the safeguard-defeated window; relevant to override control with moderate consequence tied to duration.
rows.sat_rat: Timed auto-revert of overrides is uncommon, so excessive override duration is a frequent, poorly controlled lapse. Overrides are commonly left active longer than the approved activity because there is rarely an automatic timeout. Ending an override on time depends on operator memory and discipline rather than an engineered cutoff. Prolonged overrides risk contamination cost but carry no direct metered spend.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of unintended air leakage through the chamber boundary during transfer
rows.imp_rat: Unintended air leakage through the boundary during transfer erodes separation; relevant to containment integrity but usually a gradual, partial degradation, moderating severity.
rows.sat_rat: Good seals limit leakage well when new, but gasket wear produces gradual, hard-to-notice leakage that recurs between maintenance cycles. Leakage occurs passively and imposes no direct time burden on the executor. Preventing leakage depends on door design and gasket condition rather than executor skill during transfer. Gasket replacement and seal maintenance are recurring consumable costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of chamber state leaving required limits before transfer initiation
rows.imp_rat: Chamber state leaving required limits before transfer initiation risks starting non-conforming; relevant readiness concern with moderate consequence if other gates catch it.
rows.sat_rat: Pressure interlocks are reliable for their parameter, but broader chamber-state limits (humidity, particulate, recovery time) are inconsistently interlocked, leaving gaps. Chamber recovery to target state before initiation imposes a wait that is monitored but not eliminated. Operators must still recognize when chamber state has drifted, relying on gauge reading rather than automated gating in many installations. Continuous pressure and state monitoring is standard and low-cost on modern airlocks.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration that a chamber door remains open during transfer
rows.imp_rat: Duration a door remains open sets the exposure window for the boundary; relevant to containment with severity scaling by open time, generally moderate for a single door.
rows.sat_rat: Auto-closers and timers exist on some systems but many chambers still leave open-time to operator discipline, so consistency varies. Door-open duration is directly the delay in question, and today it depends largely on how quickly the operator works. Minimizing open time relies on trained habit and efficient staging rather than any automatic control. Prolonged openings waste conditioned air and energy but the direct cost is minor.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a transfer load obstructs complete closure of a chamber access barrier
rows.imp_rat: A transfer load obstructing complete closure of a barrier prevents sealing; relevant to containment integrity with high consequence if a door cannot close.
rows.sat_rat: Door-position sensors and interlocks prevent operation when a load obstructs closure, though soft or partial obstructions can still be missed. Door-closed sensors confirm closure quickly without adding transfer time. Proper load placement to allow closure depends on straightforward operator care. Closure-detection sensors are inexpensive and standard on interlocked doors.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of adjacent-zone classifications or pressure baselines identified incorrectly in the pressure-cascade requirements
rows.imp_rat: Incorrectly identified adjacent-zone classifications or baselines in the cascade requirements corrupt the design basis; relevant to correct specification with high consequence if wrong values drive the cascade.
rows.sat_rat: Classification data is generally documented and cross-checked, so misidentification is uncommon though it depends on document accuracy. Verifying zone classifications and pressure baselines is a bounded data-check against the classification drawings. Correctly reading classification and baseline data requires familiarity with the facility scheme but is not deeply specialized. An incorrect baseline caught late is costly, but the verification itself is cheap.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of chamber boundary surfaces left inadequately decontaminated before integrity verification
rows.imp_rat: Inadequately decontaminated boundary surfaces before integrity verification undermine the cleanliness assumption; relevant to preparation with moderate consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Biological indicator placement and enzyme indicators cover many surfaces, but occluded geometries and shadowed zones commonly remain under-decontaminated and are a recurring gap. Ensuring full surface decontamination before integrity checks lengthens turnaround as hard-to-reach areas need extra exposure. Knowing which surfaces are inadequately reached requires deep process and gas-distribution expertise that many teams lack. Extra cycles, indicators and rework to cover shadowed surfaces add real recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of material leaking or spilling inside the chamber
rows.imp_rat: Material leaking or spilling inside the chamber contaminates it and possibly onward transfers; somewhat relevant to transfer control with moderate consequence, containable within the chamber.
rows.sat_rat: Chambers rarely have leak containment or detection beyond visual, so spills inside the chamber remain a largely unaddressed recurring risk. Cleaning a spill inside the chamber halts transfers and adds significant downtime. Preventing and responding to leaks relies on careful manual handling and operator response rather than engineered safeguards. Spills waste material and consume decontamination resources and chamber downtime.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unresolved conflicts among adjacent-zone classifications, room-pressure targets, and transfer requirements
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved conflicts among classifications, pressure targets, and transfer requirements are design-consistency defects; relevant to correct cascade specification with moderate consequence, usually caught pre-operation.
rows.sat_rat: Design reviews and QRA catch many conflicts, but no systematic reconciliation method exists, so unresolved conflicts recur. Resolving conflicts among classifications, pressure targets and transfer needs requires multi-party review meetings that extend timelines. Reconciling these competing requirements demands senior integration judgement across disciplines. Unresolved conflicts surfacing late drive expensive redesign.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of material containers with sterile-barrier damage from chamber passage
rows.imp_rat: Sterile-barrier damage from chamber passage compromises product but is a material-handling concern; peripheral to boundary control with moderate-high consequence for the item.
rows.sat_rat: Passage handling still causes barrier damage with no engineered protection specific to it, making this a frequently realized defect. Re-wrapping or rejecting containers with sterile-barrier damage adds rework time to the transfer. Recognizing subtle sterile-barrier damage from passage demands trained inspection that is error-prone. Damaged sterile barriers scrap the container and its contents, a direct material loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of chamber pressure deviation from transfer limits at the readiness decision
